summary: Justin Brennan explains the fundamentals of commercial multifamily financing, contrasting it with residential deals. He clarifies that lenders underwrite the property first and the borrower second, requiring minimum net worth equal to the loan amount and liquidity of 1-2% of the loan. He covers typical debt-to-equity ratios (70-30, 65-35, 60-40) and advocates graduating into larger deal sizes rather than jumping straight to massive acquisitions.
takeaways: Commercial lenders underwrite the property first, then the borrower second—not individual credit like residential
Lender requirements: net worth ≥ loan amount + liquidity of 1-2% of loan amount
If you lack liquidity/net worth, bring a Key Principal (KP) to co-sign and meet requirements
Bridge debt structure: acquisition + renovation dollars over 24-36 months, then refinance into fixed-rate (Freddie/Fannie) debt
Banks evaluate your track record and require experienced key principals on deals—your resume matters
Graduate into deal sizes progressively; smaller mistakes on smaller deals beats catastrophic failure on large deals

skill_candidates.change: Understand commercial lender underwriting hierarchy and qualifying criteria to structure deals appropriately
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explicitly teaches that commercial lenders underwrite property first, then borrower, with specific net worth (≥ loan amount) and liquidity (1-2%) thresholds; mentions Key Principal solution for those lacking capital

skill_candidates.change: Recognize financing structures (bridge vs. fixed-rate debt) and appropriate debt-to-equity ratios for different deal types
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin provides concrete ratio ranges (70-30, 65-35, 60-40) and explains bridge debt timing (24-36 months) before refinancing into permanent debt

skill_candidates.change: Graduate progressively into larger deal sizes based on track record rather than jumping to maximum deal size
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin advises against 'go big or go home' mentality, emphasizing that banks want proven track record on key principals and smaller early mistakes prevent 'one and done' catastrophic losses on large deals

title: Lesson 1: Fundamentals Of Financing
